title: The Initial Stages of Copper Deposition on Bare and Chemically Modified Gold Electrodes
abstract:
phys. stat. sol. (a) 173, 51 (1999)
Subject classification: 68.45.Da; 61.16.Ch; 68.55.Jk; S1.3
The Initial Stages of Copper Deposition
on Bare and Chemically Modified Gold Electrodes
M.A. Schneeweiss and D.M. Kolb1)
Department of Electrochemistry, University of Ulm, D-89069 Ulm, Germany
(Received September 20, 1998)
This paper comprises, in the form of an overview, various aspects of the initial stages of electrolytic
metal deposition, demonstrated and exemplified by the classical model system, Cu on Au(hkl).
Describing underpotential as well as overpotential deposition, some of the parameters governing
nucleation and growth are pointed out. The profound effect of substrate orientation is shown and
various examples of the influence of organic additives on the deposition process are given. Most
recently, metal deposition onto electrodes covered with self-assembled monolayers has received
increased attention as a possible model system for metal deposition onto a non-conducting sur-
face and therefore, preliminary results for Cu deposition on alkanethiol covered Au(111) are
presented.
1. Introduction
Much has been said about copper deposition on Au(111), which to date is still inargu-
ably one of the most popular model systems for those interested in the basics of metal
deposition. The chemically inert and structurally well-characterized substrate may be
one reason for this, making the samples easy to prepare and comparatively uncompli-
cated to handle. In this system, the formation of the first Cu monolayer on gold at
underpotentials as well as the initial stages of bulk deposition and the formation of
thicker copper films have been studied in depth and this has added to the under-
standing of metal deposition in general. In this paper, we present an overview on the
subject, focussing on work that has been done in our group during the past few
years.
A few introductory remarks about metal deposition may be useful (for a detailed
and recent review see, for example, ref. [1]): Besides being a process of wide-ranging
industrial eminence, the metal deposition process is still of great interest in terms of
basic research since a number of interfacial phenomena can be investigated in this con-
text: ordered adsorption and structural transitions in adlayers, surface diffusion, hetero-
geneous nucleation and crystal growth, epitaxial growth of overlayers and so on. In the
past twenty years, all these phenomena have been studied in great detail with the help
of model systems, employing single crystal electrodes as substrates with well-defined
surface structures. A cornerstone of electrolytic metal deposition is the so-called Nernst
equation, which describes the dependence of the equilibrium potential Er for the metal±

M.A. Schneeweiss and D.M. Kolb: Initial Stages of Copper Deposition on Au Electrodes

;
where E0 is the standard potential of the Me/Mez redox couple and aMez and aMe are
the activities of the metal ion in solution and of the metal, respectively, the latter being
unity for a pure phase. For most cases the metal ion concentration may be used in
place of the activity. If a potential is applied to the electrode which is positive of Er,
oxidative dissolution of the metal occurs; for potentials negative of Er, metal ions in the
solution are reduced, the process continuing until the metal ion concentration satisfies
the Nernst equation for the new equilibrium potential. The onset of deposition may be
delayed and hence shifted to more negative potentials due to kinetic effects such as
crystallization overpotentials or other energetic barriers.
Bulk deposition, i.e. deposition at overpotentials, can be loosely described to consist
of a nucleation and growth process, the growth of small two-dimensional or three-di-
mensional clusters, and finally the formation of a thicker overlayer, the first two steps
depending strongly on the substrate topography. The initial stages of metal deposition
onto a foreign substrate usually proceed via one of the following three growth modes;
pure
three-dimensional
growth
(``Volmer-Weber
growthº),
layer-by-layer
growth
(``Frank-van-der-Merwe growthº) or an initial layer-by-layer growth followed by three-
dimensional growth (``Stranski-Krastanov growthº) [1].
When a metal M is deposited onto a foreign metal substrate S (e.g., Cu onto Au
instead of Cu onto Cu), a phenomenon called underpotential deposition (UPD) is fre-
quently observed [2]. In that case deposition starts at potentials that are positive of the
Nernst potential for the corresponding bulk deposition up to one complete monolayer
of M on S. UPD is simply the consequence of an interaction between substrate and
deposit metal which exceeds that between the atoms of the deposit. It is easily shown
that the potential difference between UPD and OPD processes is a direct measure for
the difference in the free enthalpies of adsorption of M on S and M on M [2]. In a
more formal way UPD is described through the Nernst equation by assigning to the
monolayer of M on S an activity that is smaller than unity.
In this paper, we instrumentalize the afore-mentioned system (copper deposition on
Au(111) in sulfuric acid electrolyte) to illustrate some basic issues in the study of the
initial stages of metal deposition. Besides exemplifying the influence of anions on UPD
and the impact of substrate orientation, we explore the effect of some organic additives.
The latter can either be present in the electrolyte, in the role of the classical plating
agent, or confined to the surface, creating a chemically modified electrode. The former
may be from a variety of compound classes (we will refer here to the dye crystal violet,
thiourea, which is a sulfur-containing molecule, and sodium dodecyl sulfate, a classical
surfactant), whereas the latter nowadays are mostly thiols if the substrate is gold (in
our case, we will look at ethanethiol, a short chain thiol) and are best known as self-
assembled monolayers (SAMs). We have employed classical electrochemical methods
such as cyclic voltammetry as well as in-situ scanning tunneling microscopy (STM).
Our overview is not meant to be complete ±± its aim is rather to highlight and exem-
plify the diversified issues of the subject based on examples from our group. Experi-
mental procedures have been described in detail in a number of recent publications
[3, 4].

Bulk deposition, i.e. deposition at overpotentials, can be loosely described to consist

M.A. Schneeweiss and D.M. Kolb
2.1 Cyclic voltammetry and STM measurements
The deposition of the first Cu monolayer on Au(111) at underpotentials in sulfuric acid
electrolyte has been studied in great detail, is very well described in the literature and
shall therefore be summarized here only in brief. The Cu UPD on Au(111) is illustrated
in Fig. 1, showing the well-known cyclic voltammogram and an image of the gold elec-
trode obtained at a potential between peak A and B.
The formation of the Cu monolayer consists of two energetically separated steps
clearly discernible in the cyclic voltammogram by two current peaks A and B [5 to 9].
In the first step (peak A), an ordered layer of Cu atoms and coadsorbed sulfate ions is
) R30 structure was first observed in ex-situ LEED
The Initial Stages of Copper Deposition on Modified Gold Electrodes
Fig. 1. a) Cyclic current±potential curve for the UPD of Cu on Au(111) in 0.05 M H2SO4 + 1 mM
CuSO4. Scanning rate: 1 mV/s. b) STM image of Au(111) at 150 mV versus Cu/Cu++ in 0.1 M
and RHEED experiments [10], and later confirmed by in-situ XRD [11], STM
[12 to 15] and AFM [16]. EQCM (electrochemical quartz crystal microbalance) meas-
urements proved coadsorption of sulfate [17] and X-ray diffraction data [11] showed
the structure to consist of a honeycomb of Cu atoms at 2/3 coverage and sulfate mole-
cules adsorbed at the honeycomb centers at 1/3 coverage, results which are corro-
borated by theoretical work [18 to 21]. Nowadays, with the arrival of commercial STM
and the routine use of that technique, the (
) R30 coadsorption structure (Fig. 1b)
is widely employed as a standard in electrochemical STM. In the second step (peak B),
a full monolayer of copper (1  1) on gold is formed [3, 10]. (Recent measurements on
extremely well-oriented surfaces have revealed that the peaks may be split due to
slightly different adsorption energies at defects (step edges) and terraces [22].) In the
light of a strong anion influence on the UPD process (to be discussed below), it is
important to keep in mind that the (
) R30 structure is observed only in sul-
2.2 Current transients and adsorption kinetics
In this context a glance at the kinetics of the Cu monolayer formation and dissolution
seems appropriate, since the shape of the current peaks in Fig. 1 (particularly of peaks
B and B0) suggest that monolayer formation occurs via two-dimensional phase transi-
tions rather than mere adsorption. A conclusive answer to this question can be ob-
tained from current transients in the corresponding potential step experiments. Fig. 2
[9] shows current transients for peaks A, A0, B and B0 (as defined in Fig. 1), i.e. cur-
M.A. Schneeweiss and D.M. Kolb
Fig. 2. Current transients for the two-dimensional phase transitions during Cu UPD on Au(111) in
0.05 M H2SO4 + 1 mM CuSO4. The potential steps are indicated in the figure [9]
rent±time curves recorded after potential jumps across the peaks. We note that poten-
tial steps across peaks B, B0 and A0 all show rising transients, which is a clear indication
of a nucleation-and-growth process (the current spike at t = 0 relates to the so-called
double layer charging). This strongly suggests that the structural transitions in the un-
derpotential region can be treated as nucleation processes with subsequent two-dimen-
sional growth. STM studies have revealed that monoatomic high steps of the substrate
surface act as nucleation centers [3]. Only for peak A (relating to the transition of a
disordered copper adlayer to the (
) R30 structure) a monotonously falling
current answer is obtained (Fig. 2) which makes it difficult to decide whether formation
) R30 structure is mainly adsorption or whether it is a diffusion-lim-
If the potential stepwidth (difference between starting and final potential) is in-
creased, the surface reaction becomes faster and the current maximum is shifted to
shorter times and higher current densities while the area underneath the curves re-
mains constant (explicitely shown for peak B0 in Fig. 2). With completion of the struc-
ture transition, the current should drop to zero ±± this is indeed the case except for
peak B, where a slight leak current is found (which has been ascribed to the side reac-
tion Cu++ + e±± to Cu+). The theory by Bewick, Fleischmann and Thirsk (BFT) [23]
offers an elegant way to distinguish between instantaneous and progressive nucleation
by analyzing current transients. HoÈlzle et al. [9] made use of this method and found
that the falling part of the transients follows the model of instantaneous nucleation
(except for peak B; this deviation is ascribed to the afore-mentioned leak current),
whereas the rising part of the transients agrees to none of the two models. Deviations
from the existing theoretical models were explained by the presence of surface defects
on a real electrode in contrast to the ideally defect-free surface of the theoretical model
(which was derived for a mercury electrode). By including an adsorption term in the
BFT theory to account for Cu deposition at defects, the experimentally obtained transi-
ents could be satisfactorily reproduced. Generally, it should be kept in mind that all
processes related to metal deposition are strongly determined by the defect density of
Figure 3 illustrates the profound influence of the anions on the underpotential deposi-
tion of Cu on Au(111) [24, 25]. The cyclic voltammograms which in essence reflect the
adsorption isotherms of the Cu adlayers, clearly reveal that the anions of the support-
ing electrolyte have a strong impact on the adlayer structure as well as the deposition
kinetics. This implies that the anions are co-adsorbed with the Cu atoms which has
been proven by in-situ radiotracer [26], ex-situ AES [27], in-situ EXAFS [28, 29] and
coulometric measurements [30]. In a systematic study by LEED and RHEED it was
demonstrated that the anion is structure-determining for Cu on Au(111), the superstruc-
ture pattern for medium Cu coverage being distinctly different for perchlorate, sulfate
or chloride solutions, and differing altogether from the adlayer structure of Cu evapo-
rated onto Au(111) in UHV. (The respective structures are (2.2  2.2), (
and (5  5) in LEED notation, whereas in UHV pseudomorphic island growth is ob-
served.) Equally remarkable is the anion influence on the deposition kinetics of Cu,
reflected in the current densities of peak A. Cu deposition is extremely slow in perchlo-
The Initial Stages of Copper Deposition on Modified Gold Electrodes
rate solution, where formation of a full monolayer will require as much as 10 to 20 min.
Since the deposition rate is vastly increased by addition of chloride, the cyclic voltam-
mogram in HClO4 (Fig. 3a) will react sensitively to trace impurities of Cl±±.
Chloride is a strongly adsorbing anion and has been shown by ESCA measurements
to coadsorb with copper in the UPD range [31]. The presence of chloride shifts the
onset of deposition to more positive potentials, as seen in Fig. 3c. At potentials negative
of the first peak at 265 mV, a (5  5) structure has been reported [15] which is attri-
buted to an ordered chloride overlayer. Chloride as well as bromide (Fig. 3d) is
found to form double layers with the copper [32, 33] the former being adsorbed on
top of the UPD layer. Bromide forms a (
) R19.1 structure at potentials of
around 100 mV, which rearranges into a commensurable (4  4) structure at about
80 mV, both of which were observed in STM measurements [34, 35].
M.A. Schneeweiss and D.M. Kolb
Fig. 3. Cyclic voltammograms for the UPD of Cu on Au(111) in a) 0.1 M HClO4, b) 0.05 M
H2SO4, c) 0.1 M HClO4 + 1 mM NaCl, d) 0.1 M HClO4 + 1 mM NaBr. Copper concentration in
all cases: 1 mM. Scanning rate: 5 mV/s [25]
3.1 Bulk copper deposition on Au(111)
With the invention of STM and its application in electrochemistry it became possible to
monitor the initial stages of metal deposition in-situ. Again, copper deposition on
Au(111) [15, 36 to 38], Au(100) [39], and on polycrystalline Au [40] was among the first
systems to be studied. Nucleation at monoatomic high steps or kink sites was to be
expected from energetic considerations; and indeed copper was shown to be deposited
preferentially on defect sites.
A typical example of such behaviour is shown in Fig. 4a [3]. The first image (scan
area 100  100 nm2) depicts the Au(111) surface prior to deposition at a potential of
100 mV versus Cu/Cu++. An atomically flat terrace and an area of high step density
(step bunching) are clearly discernible. The following image was acquired after a poten-
The Initial Stages of Copper Deposition on Modified Gold Electrodes
Fig. 4. a) STM images of Au(111) in 5 mM H2SO4 + 0.05 mM CuSO4 at +0.1 V and ±±0.185 V
versus Cu/Cu++. Nucleation and growth occurs almost exclusively at step edges. b) Au(111) in
0.1 M H2SO4 + 1 mM CuSO4 at 0.15 and ±±0.3 V, respectively. At higher overpotentials nucleation
and growth sets in also on terraces
tial step to ±±185 mV, displaying instantaneous nucleation and the formation of mesa-
like crystallites with atomically flat top layers, which often show triangular symmetry. It
is clear that under these conditions nucleation takes place almost exclusively at step
edges, an effect that has been shown in numerous publications [3, 15, 36, 37, 39]. At
higher overpotentials, however, growth begins on terraces as well as at step edges,
which can be seen in Fig. 4b. This shows a scan area of 178  178 nm2 prior to (top-
view) and during (3D plot) deposition. The dashed white line indicates the precise mo-
ment of the potential step to ±±300 mV. In this case, the high overpotential leads to
nucleation on the terraces as well as the steps. Additionally, the rapid formation of
much larger and higher clusters is evident (note the different height scale of a) and b)).
The growth mechanism, however, is essentially independent of the overvoltage. The
deposition of a full monolayer of copper at underpotentials (see Section 2) is followed
by three-dimensional growth; this conforms to what is generally known as Stranski-
Krastanov growth (layer by layer growth followed by three-dimensional growth).
The growth of a single crystallite was further investigated by so-called x±t scans
[15]. Such an STM image is acquired by scanning repeatedly one and the same line
in x-direction. The vertical scale thus becomes a time scale and the line scan represents
a cross section through the growing cluster. Such a measurement, displayed as a three-
dimensional image, is shown in Fig. 5; the scanline is 150 nm wide and the time scale
spans 120 s. In this case, a so-called ``tip-induced clusterº [41 to 43] was created at
about t = 10 s to serve as a nucleation site and to make sure growth would indeed take
M.A. Schneeweiss and D.M. Kolb
Fig. 5. Growth of an individual Cu cluster on Au(111) at ±±180 mV versus Cu/Cu++ followed over
a period of about 2 min by monitoring its cross-section with STM [15] 0.5 M H2SO4 + 5 mM
place underneath the tip. The electrode potential was ±±185 mV versus Cu/Cu++. After
an induction period of about 15 s the growth sets in, causing two layers of bulk copper
to grow almost simultaneously. The growth of each of the following 12 layers can be
clearly distinguished. As is seen in Fig. 5, the first Cu layer which grows at overpoten-
tials (and which is already the second Cu layer on the gold substrate because of UPD),
spreads across the surface much slower than the following layers, thus limiting the lat-
eral growth rate of the crystallite. This growth behaviour leads to the formation of a
mesa-like cluster, a shape that was observed already in Fig. 4. The slow growth of the
first bulk layer may relate to the fact that it is deposited on a Cu monolayer which is
pseudomorphic with the gold substrate, i.e. which has the lattice constants of gold
rather than bulk Cu. However, it is important to note that x and z-directions in this
image have very different scalings; overall growth in z-direction is only 3.2 nm
(14 monoatomic layers in 95 s), whereas the lateral growth proceeds much faster. It is
not advisable to treat rate constants acquired by this method as absolute; it has often
been observed that under tunneling conditions the mere presence of the the tip can
strongly affect the kinetics of a deposition process.
Continuing growth of copper leads to the development of thick copper crystals, ter-
minated by (111) facets parallel to the gold substrate. On top of these crystals, a distinc-
tive moireÂ pattern due to sulfate adsorbed on Cu(111) [44 to 46] is found. Longer
deposition times eventually lead to surface alloying [40]. After oxidative stripping of
the Cu overlayer, the terraces are covered with monoatomic deep pits and monoatomic
high islands (cf. Fig. 8). Redeposition of Cu will lead to a different morphology because
nucleation and growth depends on the defect density of the substrate. If the formerly
smooth terraces are now covered with pits and islands, all of these will act as nuclea-
tion centers and thus produce far more nuclei and, subsequently, crystallites.
The bulk Cu deposition on Au(100) [47] shows some very interesting variances from the
same process on Au(111), proving the profound influence of the substrate orientation
on the growth mechanism. The growth in this system does not follow a Stranski-Krasta-
nov behaviour (three-dimensional cluster formation on preformed UPD layer), but
rather a Frank-van der Merwe growth (i.e. layer-by-layer). Even more surprising is the
observation that copper grows pseudomorphically onto Au(100) up to several layers,
despite a large lattice mismatch of about 13%. Fig. 6 shows a sequence of STM images
(scan range 220  220 nm2) which starts with a Cu-free Au(100) substrate (islands are
due to lifting of reconstruction, see e.g. ref. [48]). Detailed measurements not shown
here [49] demonstrated that the initial growth begins with the formation of bilayers on
top of the pseudomorphic Cu UPD layer, the rims of the gold islands serving as the
preferred nucleation sites. The subsequent growth of Cu layers after a potential step
from +500 to ±±200 mV versus SCE is shown in Fig. 6, where the layers are numbered,
``1º refering to the Cu UPD layer on Au(100). Atomic-resolution STM images [49] as
well as in-situ X-ray diffraction measurements [50] have confirmed that the growing
phase is b.c.c. Cu, as has first been observed for Cu on Ag(100) in UHV [51]. Although
b.c.c. Cu is thermodynamically slightly less favourable than f.c.c. Cu, there is an almost
perfect lattice match between b.c.c. Cu and Au(100) which seems to revert the ener-
getics for a Cu overlayer on Au(100) in favour of the b.c.c. modification.
The Initial Stages of Copper Deposition on Modified Gold Electrodes
M.A. Schneeweiss and D.M. Kolb
Fig. 6. Sequence of consecutive STM images showing the growth of several copper layers on
Au(100) [47, 49]. 0.1 M H2SO4 + 0.5 mM CuSO4. After a potential jump from 0.5 V (part a) to
ÿ0.2 V (arrow in part b) the copper deposition follows a Frank-van der Merwe growth (parts b to
d). With the formation of the eleventh copper layer a striped structure appears
The first ten Cu layers are found to form a smooth pseudomorphic film on the sub-
strate, whereas the eleventh layer marks the onset of a transition to f.c.c.-like copper
and a striped (``wavyº) structure appears. Such a behaviour was first shown for the
analogous system Cu on Ag(100) [52], where in spite of the large lattice mismatch,
pseudomorphic growth has been observed up to the eighth layer. In that case, the
striped phase appeared with the ninth layer. Above an average of about 20 monolayers
of Cu, the striped structure disappeared and crystallites of f.c.c. Cu emerged.
4. Cu Deposition in the Presence of Plating Additives
Metal deposition in real life, as opposed to laboratory experiments, is largely conducted
in the presence of so-called plating additives [53]. These substances are also known as
``levellers and brightenersº, a term which stresses their effect on the resulting deposit.
In this context, ``levellingº refers to the production of a deposit which is relatively
thick in substrate depressions while relatively thinner on substrate protrusions [54],
while the ``brighteningº effect describes the generation of fine-grained deposit consist-
ing of particles smaller than the wavelength of visible light [55, 56]. In order to
create a truly bright surface though, it is further neccessary that the small particles be
crystalline and oriented with a light-reflecting plane parallel to the substrate surface
Whereas a few additives work both as levellers and brighteners, most fulfil only one
demand and thus in industrial plating baths usually a mixture of several additives is
used. Despite their importance for industrial processes and despite a fair number of
basic studies available in the literature, the exact mechanism is still not completely un-
derstood for every additive. There are several classes of additives, which work by differ-
ent mechanisms (for review articles, see e.g. [53, 59 to 61]) and which can be categorized
in various ways according to chemical nature, mode of action and selectivity. Some of
the proposed mechanisms according to Franklin [61] include surface blocking mechan-
isms [53, 62] (strongly adsorbing additives with hydroxyl, thiol, disulfide and thioether
groups [63]); changes in the potential drop across the double layer (caused by ionic
additives); complex formation (by complexing agents), thereby lowering the concentra-
tion of the ion and causing a shift in the Nernst potential; enhanced adsorption of the
complexed ion or introduction of a bridging ligand [64]; ion pairing [65] (synergistic
effect, for example using a quarternary salt); changes in interfacial tension (surfactants)
and film formation on the electrode; formation of insoluble films on the electrode sur-
Systematic studies on metal deposition in the presence of additives (various systems)
have been conducted by classical electrochemical methods (e.g. [66 to 77]) partly com-
bined with more structure-sensitive methods like confocal laser scanning microscopy
[78], laser Raman spectroscopy [79] or electrochemical quartz microbalance [80]. Some
authors used STM [3, 77, 81 to 84] or AFM [85, 86] to monitor the influence of addi-
tives on the deposition; model systems like Cu on single-crystalline Au or Pt were used
To gain basic knowledge about the working of plating agents, a well-known model
system like Cu on Au(111) can conveniently be used. Important characteristics of the
additive-free case, like the three-dimensional growth mechanism and instantaneous nu-
cleation, have been described above.
The Initial Stages of Copper Deposition on Modified Gold Electrodes
Fig. 7a shows again the typical appearance of the initial growth phase of copper on
gold in the absence of any additive [3]: Nucleation at surface defects and three-dimen-
sional cluster growth, leading to a rough Cu overlayer. When crystal violet (CV), a
cationic dye, is added to the solution, nucleation is still seen to occur at defects such as
monoatomic high steps, but the growth behaviour of Cu is vastly changed (Fig. 7b).
A quasi-two-dimensional growth is observed and deposition of a few monolayers only
is needed to cover completely the whole substrate surface. The price to pay for a fa-
M.A. Schneeweiss and D.M. Kolb
Fig. 7. Comparison of Cu deposits on Au(111) formed a) in the absence of organic additive, b) in
the presence of crystal violet (CV) and c) in the presence of thiourea (TU) [3]. a) E = ±±0.15 V.
5 mM H2SO4 + 0.05 mM CuSO4, b) E = ±±0.13 V. 0.05 M H2SO4 + 1 mM CuSO4 + 0.1 mM CV,
c) E = ±±0.10 V. 5 mM H2SO4 + 0.05 mM CuSO4 + 0.1 mM TU. All potentials versus Cu/Cu++
vourable spreading of the deposit on the surface is a severe inhibition of the growth
normal to it. In other words, the overall deposition rate for Cu is markedly reduced in
the presence of crystal violet. This leads us to conclude that CV adsorbs on top of the
Cu mesas, thus lowering the growth rate normal to the surface and hence allowing the
spread parallel to the surface to compete. Finally, Fig. 7c shows the effect of thiourea
on the Cu deposition, a molecule which (by the strong interaction of Au and S) is
bound much stronger to the gold surface than CV. Thiourea is a widely used additive
especially in nickel plating baths [87]. The kinetics of the Cu bulk deposition is strongly
affected, and a marked increase of the deposition rate with thiourea concentration is
found. Again, this system shows progressive nucleation [77]. In contrast to the former
two examples, it seems as if Cu is nucleating on the terraces of the gold substrate
rather than on step edges. A detailed investigation, however, has revealed that this is
not the case. In the presence of thiourea, the UPD of Cu is kinetically hindered and
the Cu adlayer grows slowly in patches on the thiourea-covered gold surface. STM
studies have shown that the rims of the Cu UPD islands act as nucleation centers for
bulk Cu deposition, leaving the impression of Cu clusters growing on substrate terraces
Sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) belongs to the group of surfactants which is frequently
used in industrial plating baths with the aim of increasing wetting and additionally re-
moving impurities. In many cases, surfactant molecules have been found to adsorb on
solid surfaces forming so-called hemimicelles, i.e. hemi-cylinders or half cylindrical ag-
gregates which form regular line patterns on the surface [88 to 90].
Fig. 8 shows a Au(111) electrode in 0.5 M H2SO4 + 0.4 mM CuSO4 + 0.1 M SDS at
130 mV versus Cu/Cu++ [91]. At this potential the surface is covered by a layer of SDS

Continuous deposition of Cu leads to relatively large, well-defined crystallites (Fig. 9).
Adsorbed molecules on the surface seem to block nucleation on terraces; Cu deposition
on Au(111) in the presence of SDS originates solely at defects, preferably at step
5. Cu Deposition on Surface Confined Layers (Thiols)
A number of compound classes spontaneously form organized adlayers, so-called ``self-
assembled monolayersº (SAMs) on certain substrates (see e.g. review article [92]). One
of the most important systems of this kind is comprised of a gold substrate (usually
Au(111)) and a thiol adlayer. The bulk of publications on the subject deals with alkane
thiols (e.g. [93, 94]) and their structure (e.g. [95 to 100] and review article [101], which
places emphasis on STM studies of the subject). In contrast to the complex working of
plating additives the influence of the surface-confined SAM can be regarded as limited
M.A. Schneeweiss and D.M. Kolb

10±±4 M SDS after 20 min deposition at ±±0.17 V versus Cu/Cu++ [91]
to the factor of surface blocking. The dense and well-defined SAM can function as a
model system for metal deposition on non-conductors and in a wider sense is hoped to
enhance understanding on organic/inorganic interfaces.
